text: The Political Economy of Neoliberal Narrativeimothy Besley LSE Adam Brzezinski LSE November 28, 2024 Abstract This paper develops a canonical approach to incorporating narratives into standard models of political competition.
Politicians supply narratives to win elections, and voters demand narratives that suit their interests, based on motivated reasoning.
We apply this framework to study the rise of neoliberal narratives that emphasise the trickle-down benefits of deregulation.
We provide insights into why neoliberalism gained traction following the 1970s oil shocks, and how it evolved into a new consensus narrative, sometimes called the Third Way.
Our framework shows how temporary economic shocks can lead to a narrative dynamic that permanently alters policy choices.

∗ We are grateful to Roland Bénabou, Jonathan Bendor, Steve Callander, Paul Collier, Matt Gentzkow, Larry Kramer, Gilat Levy, Itzhak Rasooly, Andrei Shleifer, Steven Teles, David Tuckett, Stephane Wolton and a number of seminar participants at LSE, King’s College, Stanford, University of Nottingham, and the IOG-BFI conference for helpful comments and insights.
Narratives that are used for interpreting how the world works have long been seen as crucial for shaping policy debates.
Discussions of economic policy problems are frequently communicated to citizens via narratives in order to influence their voting behavior and thereby to affect policy choices.
Yet, until recently, policy narratives were largely ignored by theoretical frameworks in political economy despite increasing interest in their role among economists.
This paper develops a canonical approach for bringing narratives into standard models of political competition, following an emerging literature that models narratives as subjective causal models.
In our framework, voters demand narratives to make sense of the world and to inform their policy preferences.
They look at the set of available narratives, adopting the one that they find most appealing, based on motivated reasoning.
Politicians supply narratives in political campaigns, communicating them strategically to influence how voters behave in order to gain an electoral advantage.
The set of narratives that voters adopt influences which policy platforms are ex-post credible.
In political equilibrium, politicians will deploy new narratives only if they lead to policy convictions that improve their electoral chances.

Our framework allows us to explore a new dimension of policy disagreement driven by narrative polarization, where groups of citizens endogenously choose competing causal models of how policy affects economic outcomes.

This enriches political disagreement beyond standard economic interests and allows us to capture Keynes’ famous view expressed in the concluding paragraph of his General Theory of Employment, Interest, and Money that “the power of vested interests is vastly exaggerated compared with the gradual encroachment of ideas.. sooner or later, it is ideas, not vested interests, which are dangerous for good or evil.” And it chimes with the views of narrative psychologists that the human mind absorbs narratives to supplement logic and evidence-based thinking.

To illustrate the utility of the framework, we employ it to explore the rise of a particular policy narrative, often referred to as neoliberalism, a worldview that emphasises the virtues of free markets.
Neoliberalism is now often used as a pejorative term, though it did not originate as such; indeed, some of its leading proponents proudly self-identified as neoliberals.

Among these was Milton Friedman, who wrote that “neo-liberalism offers a real hope of a better future, a hope that is already a strong cross-current of opinion and that is capable of capturing the enthusiasm of men of good-will everywhere, and thereby becoming the major current of opinion”. A contemporaneous example 1 of self-identification with neoliberalism is the Adam Smith Institute in the UK, which in 2016 started to use the term to describe its policy views. 1 In this paper, we refrain from taking a normative stance for or against policies referred to as neoliberal.

Instead, we analyse the conditions under which neoliberal narratives thrive. 2 This, we argue, depends on two factors: whether neoliberal narratives are demanded by voters, and whether they are supplied by parties.
On the demand-side, neoliberal narratives will only be popular if they give hope of a better future to (some) voters, as suggested in the quote by Friedman above.
On the supply side, neoliberal narratives will be deployed by parties if that leads to an electoral advantage, which only happens under specific conditions.
In order to investigate the political success of neoliberalism, our framework gives a precise definition of the term.

Specifically, neoliberal narratives are causal policy models with two key features: (i) they highlight the trickle-down benefits of policies which harm poorer individuals, arguing that losses will be more than offset by gains accruing from a larger size of the economy; and (ii) they question the ability of government to produce public goods efficiently.

The intellectual history of neoliberal thought stretches back to von Mises, Hayek, and other Austrian economists, amidst the fall of the Austro-Hungarian empire after WWI. They became common currency with the rise of the Chicago School, notably under the influence of Milton Friedman.

Yet, for the most part, these ideas remained on the fringe of policy thinking, until they became influential following the oil crises of the 1970s and the subsequent rise to power of Margaret Thatcher and Ronald Reagan.

Neoliberalism fractured a policy consensus that had emerged following the Great Depression and World War II, one that argued for a wider role for government in the form of demand management and regulation of markets.
This post-war consensus travels under a variety of names.

In the US, Gerstle refers to it as “the New Deal Order”. In the UK, it is associated with the term “Butskellism”, coined by The Economist magazine in 1954 to describe the convergence of views across the Labour and Conservative parties.
Under this consensus, policies of parties from the left and right frequently resembled each other.
In the US, Eisenhower, a Republican president, embraced most of the policy platforms of Franklin D. Roosevelt.
In the UK, the Conservative party switched to supporting the National Health Service in the same time period.

While there were dissenters in both cases, policy continuity remained the norm as adminstrations turned over. 1 See https://www.adamsmith.org/blog/coming-out-as-neoliberals. 2 A related paper is Bénabou, which offers a model of different possible ideologies about the scope of state intervention versus free markets.
That the rise of Margaret Thatcher and Ronald Reagan came with new policy narratives is hard to dispute.

An iconic example of the shift in emphasis can be found in Ronald Reagan’s inaugural address in 1981: We invented the assembly line and mass production, but punitive tax policies and excessive and unnecessary regulations plus government borrowing have stifled our ability to update plant and equipment.
When capital investment is made, it’s too often for some unproductive alterations demanded by government to meet various of its regulations.
Reagan’s shift away from interventionism was a clear departure from views that had dominated policy-thinking in preceding decades.
Moreover, the associated policies gained support from voters with both Reagan and Thatcher going on to be re-elected, in the latter case twice.
Neoliberalism also fared well elsewhere in the same time period and the “Washington Consensus” became an established blueprint for policy reforms around the world.
Policy changes such as the shift away from the post-war consensus towards neoliberalism do not come out of thin air; in democracies they are the product of a competitive political process.

In standard models of political competition where voters are assumed to have a common understanding of how the economy works, policy shifts are caused by new evidence or a change in economic interests.

As such approaches assume away the possibility that citizens have different beliefs about how policies work, they also cannot explain polarization in beliefs of the kind that dominated politics in the US and UK in the 1980s.

In contrast to this standard approach, our framework allows citizens to choose among competing narratives that combine causal parameters across the different dimensions in which policy affects outcomes into a coherent world view.
Citizens choose the narratives that maximise their expected utility, a process of motivated reasoning that can be thought of as occurring subconsciously.

Put simply, citizens believe things because they are convenient rather than because they are evidencebased, an idea that has long been accepted by psychologists, but is relatively new in economic theory.
This approach allows us to investigate situations where citizens disagree because of the subjective causal model that they use rather than because of differences in evidence.

In our application to neoliberalism, this approach can shed light on why some groups of citizens were convinced of the neoliberal approach while others were not, how this led to polarization in the policies of parties, and why a new cross-party consensus around the merits of deregulation eventually formed.
Beyond shedding light on the rise of neoliberalism, our framework offers some more general insights into how narratives shape policy.
A central take-away is that temporary economic shocks can lead to a narrative dynamic that permanently shifts policy equilibria.
This is because economic shocks can incentivize 3 parties to deploy new narratives that otherwise would not have been electorally advantageous.
These narratives become part of the public discourse, preventing the return to a previous policy equilibrium, even after the economy recovers.
We also show that “dominated” narratives, that are unanimously less preferred to another narrative, can become part of a political equilibrium.
Parties may find it optimal to supply dominated narratives precisely because these will be believed by some, but not all, citizens.
Such narrative polarization feeds into policy polarization, giving one party a temporary electoral advantage, until another party deploys a narrative that leads to a new consensus.
Our framework shows why polarizing narratives that give one party an electoral advantage, and consensus narratives which eliminate that advantage, can alternate dynamically.

Our model builds on an emerging literature in political economy that investigates competition in narratives. 3 There is now an established convention in economic theory of defining narratives as subjective causal models. 4 However, the way in which competition in narratives is modelled, and how narratives are assumed to relate to available data, varies widely.

For example, Eliaz and Spiegler assume that narratives may incorrectly specify the causal chain between variables, but that, conditional on that causal chain, beliefs are assumed to fit available data.
Schwartzstein and Sunderam posit that the most persuasive causal models are those that most closely fit the data to be explained, an idea also taken up by Aina.

And Levy et al. develop a model where voters have exogenously given narratives, some of which are incorrect; conditional on these narratives, voters update beliefs consistently with the available data.
In our framework, motivated reasoning rather than data disciplines the narratives that voters adopt.
Such reasoning is a well-established idea in psychology and has been adopted in formal models in political economy.
The idea of motivated reasoning connects to an older tradition in economic theory that questions Bayesian belief formation.
Previous approaches have highlighted the role of emotions against outside groups, social networks and group identity, and ideologies.
Although our purpose is to build a theoretical framework, it is closely allied to an emerging empirical literature that investigates narratives and related concepts.

Several tools have been developed to analyse text in political speeches (Gentzkow 3 This literature complements the widespread approach of Bayesian persuasion models. 4 Note, however, that some papers define narratives differently.
4 et al., 2019; Ash et al., 2024).

Moreover, there is increasing recognition that beliefs are not solely data-driven, but can be influenced by plot lines in soap operas, reality shows, divergent narratives in opinion programs on TV, videos of immigrant life experience, light entertainment broadcasting, and plays.
The remainder of the paper proceeds as follows.
Section 2 introduces our core model, which builds on standard approaches in political economy, but introduces the concept of narratives as alternative causal models of how policy affects outcomes.
Section 3 solves a static model for a choice between two narratives: the post-war consensus and neoliberalism.

Section 4 introduces a wider range of narratives and extends the model dynamically, so that parties can choose whether to propagate new narratives based on the way that they affect their electoral prospects.
We summarise our insights and directions for future research in section 5.
The Appendix contains the proofs and other supporting material.
Basics We begin by outlining the core model that makes precise the content of different political narratives and use it to explore the role of narratives in political competition.
Private incomes and deregulation The economy is modelled following Besley and Persson.
There are two types of citizens differentiated by their level of income indexed by K [ x, x ], where higher x corresponds to de regulation.

Tuckett and Nikolic suggest that “conviction narratives enable actors to draw on the information, beliefs, causal models, and rules of thumb situated in their social context to identify opportunities worth acting on, to simulate the future outcome of the actions by means of which they plan to achieve those opportunities, and to feel sufficiently convinced about the anticipated outcomes to act.” 7 consistent with the data.
In contrast, in our approach there is no disagreement about the direction of causation, and instead the disagreement is in the size of the relevant parameters.
Parties Policy is determined in political equilibrium by competition between two parties that represent each group.
Parties are denoted by J [ t, t ]. The two parties are staffed by lowand highincome citizen-candidates, respectively, who cannot commit to policies ex-ante.
Thus the only incentive compatible policies are those that maximise the utility of their own income type.
To chime with standard party classifications in political science, we will refer to the high income party as the Right and the low income party as the Left.

This describes one non-standard feature of the model: citizens “choose” their preferred narrative, which will influence their policy preferences and thereby the equilibrium policies of parties.
As in other models of motivated reasoning, such as Bénabou, this process of narrative adoption can be thought of as happening subconsciously.
When this narrative adoption happens as part of a political equilibrium, it will be affected by what citizens believe will be the policy outcomes.
And this, in turn, may depend on the narrative choices they think that others will make.
Our approach to political equilibrium demands that this happens in a consistent way, as we describe next.
Equilibrium Structure The timing of the model is as follows:
Citizens choose a narrative from the available set. 2.
The winning party implements policy. 4.
Payoffs are realized.
A political equilibrium comprises a pair of narratives ˆ; a set of policy choicest̂ J,x̂ J for J ∈{ H, L } that satisfy (4); and a winning probability ˆ π that satisfies (6).
In this section, we use the framework of the previous section to study neoliberal narratives.
The specificity of our modelling approach makes it possible to state precisely what we mean by “neoliberalism”, a term that is often only vaguely defined.
We will characterise the conditions for a narrative consensus or polarization across groups of citizens and show how the success of the neoliberal narrative depends on the state of the economy.

Basics To simplify the analysis, we assume that there is common tax policy across parties, i.e. t = t = t. 7 Without loss of generality, we focus exclusively on conviction narratives C that put all of their weight on one particular parameter vector.
Specifically: Proposition 1.
For any political equilibrium, there exists an equilibrium with the same policy outcomes where citizens adopt conviction narratives.
The reason for this is that if citizens adopt a non-conviction narrative in equilibrium, they must be indifferent between all parameter triplets that they assign weight to.
For each group J, at least one of these triplets will be consistent with the original equilibrium policies {x̂ J }. Given this, we will focus exclusively on conviction narratives
These conditions imply that the trickledown condition holds for the neoliberal narrative, such that x is believed to increase aggregate income, but not for the post-war consensus narrative.

In the Supplemental Appendix S, we discuss two extensions to the framework.
First, we discuss results when both deregulation x and tax policies t are endogenous.
We show conditions under which neoliberalism comes with a tax cut, leading to polarization in both deregulation and tax policies.
The main take-away of endogenising tax rates is that, although things get somewhat more complicated, the same broad conclusions can be drawn as with fixed taxes.
Divergence in equilibrium narratives and policies, which now include both taxation and regulation, occur for intermediate values of
So far, we have assumed that the set of available narratives is given exogenously.
We now explore the possibility that narratives are propagated as part of a party’s electoral strategy.

Since this leads to the set of available narratives evolving over time, we develop a dynamic (infinite horizon) version of the model where we allow political parties to propagate new narratives to determine the narrative set that citizens can choose from.
In this dynamic version of the model, narrative supply and demand are determined by the strategic interaction between politicians, who control political parties, and voters.
Narratives will only become available to voters if it is in the interest of parties to propagate them, and they will only gain traction if it is in the interest of citizens to believe in them.
So, even though many narratives are possible, not all of them will become part of a political equilibrium.
The analysis highlights different possible dynamic paths of narratives and policies.

In particular, we show that weaker economic conditions will give rise to the neoliberal narrative among high-income citizens and policy polarization across parties, before both parties converge on a new consensus narrative that stresses the merits of deregulation.
Even temporary economic shocks can unleash this narrative dynamic and permanently change the policy equilibrium.

The framework therefore exhibits a natural form of hysteresis since, once a narrative is propagated in response to an economic shock, it continues to shape policy equilibria even after the economy recovers.

The Narrative Space Since the three causal parameters “Third Way” narrative, following a term that has been used to describe the politics of Clinton, Blair, and other politicians from traditionally centre-left parties who adopted a more pro-market stance in the 1990s. 12 This is a form of “left-wing neoliberalism” that combines el Interestingly, Gerstle uses the term “Third Way” as a description of the New Deal narrative that emerged in the 1930s.
The term was also used by Wilhelm Röpke, one of the Presidents of the Mont Pelerin Society, to describe an economic order that combines marketbased approaches with some role for state intervention.

When the economy is doing less well ( ̃ ̃; this result shows that this is a global result even allowing for the set of all possible narratives to be propagated by parties.
In this case, the economy will maintain a consensus regulation policy.

The more interesting possibility is Case 2, when the economy is doing less well as represented by lower α. Now the Proposition says that one party will propagate the neoliberal narrative, and the other the Third Way narrative.
Eventually, the state will converge to one where the Third Way narrative is successfully deployed, all citizens believe in it, and there is convergence on deregulation across both parties.
However, before that happens, there is potential for the neoliberal narrative to generate polarization in citizen narratives and party policies.
To explain the logic of this result, recall that in the initial state ̃ − ( γ − — high α — makes neoliberalism unattractive.

Proposition 2 also shows that, starting from a high α, the neoliberal narrative can only break the post-war consensus if there is a downturn in the economy—a decrease in α. This corresponds to an established view that neoliberal narratives gained traction during the economic turbulence of the 1970s, an idea captured succinctly in the preface of Friedman:
“Only a crisis - actual or perceived - produces real change.
When that crisis occurs, the actions that are taken depend on the ideas that are lying around.

That, I believe, is our basic function: to develop alternatives to existing policies, to keep them alive and available until the politically impossible becomes the politically inevitable.” He is pointing out that narratives can exist in the toolkit of intellectuals, but they only become part of the political process when the conditions are right.

The neoliberal narrative was clearly known before economic turbulance hit in the 1970s but our model suggests that it was not going to gain traction even if a party deployed it in the benign economic circumstances that prevailed. 17 In contrast, 16 See the appendix for the proof to Proposition 3.
It outlines some subtleties in the reasoning.
We show that some narratives will not be employed because they achieve the same electoral outcomes while being less preferred by citizens.

One other narrative is polarizing for a lower range of α < ̃ α which we do not consider here; however, it is strictly dominated by ν n from the citizens’ perspective and therefore does not change the long-run outcomes either. 17 Of course, we do not allow economic shocks to be a function of the policies in place and doubtless some proponents of neoliberalism blamed the turbulence of the 1970s on the policies that prevailed.
However, the oil shocks of the 1970s were plausibly exogenous.

Given that parties are assumed to care about winning this is not a matter of party ideology. 18 Narrative competition therefore has some features that are familiar from models of creative destruction.
Moreover, the policy choice that any party makes simply follows what is optimal for the group that they represent.
In reality, we know that parties of the political right, the Republicans in the US and the Tories in the UK, were the vanguard of neoliberalism.
We now explore when our model predicts that this will be the case.

Our results show that temporary economic shocks can have permanent effects on policy.
To see this, observe that if initially we start with ̄

This paper has laid out a framework for studying the role of narratives as subjective causal models in a canonical model of political competition of the kind that dominates the political economy literature, where two parties from the left and right are competing for power and class differences are the key political cleavage.
We have explored static and dynamic aspects of a world in which citizens can choose which narratives to incorporate into their beliefs based on self-serving motivated reasoning.
This can result in narrative polarization where preferences for divergent policies are driven not just by interests but by differing views about how the economy works.

We have argued that this kind of framework can usefully be applied to study the emergence of neoliberal thinking and policy making that emerged in the late 1970s and has become a cornerstone of economic policy ever since.
For many commentators, this has led to policy models being sustained that are detached from reality, making some kind of motivated reasoning a plausible explanation.
While initiated by an economic shock in the 1970s, its persistence has been a source of puzzlement.

As De Long has put it: “The smart thing to say is that. neoliberalism is, or ought to be, dead, but zombie-like, it still walks the earth.” But the detachment of narrative-based beliefs from evidence can allow such persistence to emerge and to be sustained.
Our approach can also explain why a consensus formed around a particular version of neoliberalism which has been referred to as the “Third Way” since Clinton and Blair.

Although these are complex issues, we think that there is value in debating them using a modelling framework which can bring a degree of specificity and precision to what are often quite vague concepts.

In spite of the inevitable simplicity of the approach that we have suggested with only two groups, two parties and two policy dimensions, there is richness in the analysis, especially when dynamics are considered.
Bringing the theoretical approach to the data is possible using different kinds of text data.
First, it is possible to explore shifts in narratives in political speeches in legislatures and elsewhere using new methods that use machine learning to detect causal statements.
The framework that we have proposed with three elements to the core narratives can be used to frame the way in which such text data is categorized.
Second, one can look at party manifestos and the influence that neoliberalism has had on policy making through this lens.
Another area for further investigation is the nature of narrative innovation.
Our core model of eight possible narratives considers each narrative to be a re combination of existing parameters.
Hence the innovation created by the Third Way was a hybrid of the post-war consensus and the neoliberal narrative.
Thus, our dynamic model shows the scope for such narratives to have an effect on policymaking.
Another way of innovating narratives would be to create new dimensions of policy difference that voters had not been focusing on.
These diversification narratives would work by introducing new parameters into the parameter space and bringing attention to aspects of policy that other narratives have ignored.

A recent example of such narratives is the way in which arguments about security and resilience are being used to reshape debates about the consequences of economic openness, an element of neoliberalism.
The policy has not changed, only the way that its consequences are being presented to voters.
Thus, the new security and resilience narrative argues for taxes on Chinese imports on cars in the US on the grounds of reducing import dependence on a potentially hostile country.
Such narratives have the potential to break out of the Third Way consensus by providing new justifications for low x in our model.
We have modeled narratives as being isomorphic to subjective causal models without getting into their cognitive underpinnings.
However, exploring these is an important topic for future work on narratives.
Arguably what makes a narrative influential is its capacity to make specific causal features of policy salient or creating something that is memorable when voters are casting their ballots.

Kendall and Charles study a micro founded model with experimental evidence and it could be fruitful to connect this to policy case studies which could provide a tighter connection to the literature on cognition and bounded rationality in policy choice.
